case_name: 1,2-Dibromoethane removal from groundwater for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for groundwater contaminated with 1,2-dibromoethane intended for drinking water production.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipal utility operates groundwater wells in an agricultural area with legacy fumigant impacts and has detected 1,2-dibromoethane in the raw water. The utility needs a proven treatment approach that can reliably meet drinking water goals without high operator burden or excessive energy demand. Water quality is otherwise typical of mildly mineralized groundwater, but some natural organic matter is present and may affect adsorption performance.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for 1,2-dibromoethane and provide consistent potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use established and well-supported treatment technology
maintain stable operation with moderate operator attention

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: remove incidental particulate matter and protect downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb 1,2-dibromoethane from groundwater using a proven fixed-bed process
core_unit_functions.lead_lag_gac_polishing: provide breakthrough protection and stable compliance through staged carbon operation
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in distributed finished water

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is a volatile synthetic organic compound commonly addressed with activated carbon in drinking water treatment practice
the contaminant is the clear primary treatment target and source water turbidity is low, making a simple adsorption train practical
moderate DOC suggests some competitive adsorption, so lead-lag GAC configuration improves reliability and compliance protection
the process is widely understood, compatible with municipal groundwater treatment, and fits the need for stable operation
potential_risks: natural organic matter can shorten carbon bed life and increase changeout frequency
breakthrough monitoring is needed because low drinking water limits require conservative bed management
spent carbon handling or regeneration logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile 1,2-dibromoethane from water to air using packed-tower or similar stripping
core_unit_functions.off_gas_control: capture or treat stripped contaminant in compliance with air management needs
core_unit_functions.post_filtration_if_needed: polish water and remove any entrained solids if required by system configuration

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is volatile, so air stripping is a technically effective established option for groundwater VOC removal
the raw water has low particulate loading, which supports efficient tower operation
this is a recognized conventional approach when vapor-phase management can be accommodated
potential_risks: off-gas treatment may be required and adds complexity compared with adsorption-only systems
tower scaling, fouling, or airflow control issues can affect performance stability
cold weather and blower energy requirements can complicate operations

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: protect air stripping equipment from particulate accumulation
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration: remove the bulk of volatile 1,2-dibromoethane from water
core_unit_functions.vapor_phase_gac_off_gas_treatment: control contaminant released to air from the stripping system
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_gac_polishing: provide final polishing and compliance protection against residual contaminant

applicability_rationale: combined stripping plus carbon polishing can provide robust treatment where very low finished-water concentrations are required
the hybrid train adds safety margin against fluctuations in raw-water concentration and mass-transfer variability
it remains based on established VOC treatment technologies
potential_risks: higher capital and O&M burden than single-process options
more instrumentation and monitoring are needed across water and air treatment components
added complexity may be unnecessary for a straightforward moderate-strength groundwater case

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide adsorption of organic contaminants during batch or continuous contact
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: capture PAC and suspended solids into settleable or filterable flocs
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove PAC-associated solids and polish finished water

applicability_rationale: activated carbon is relevant for organic contaminant control, so PAC-based treatment is technically plausible
the approach may be considered where a utility already has conventional solids treatment infrastructure
potential_risks: PAC is generally less preferred than fixed-bed GAC for sustained removal of trace VOCs in groundwater
chemical feed control and solids residuals handling increase operational burden
performance can be less predictable for continuous compliance at very low contaminant targets

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum dissolved contaminant reduction through membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis can reduce many dissolved contaminants and may remove some organic compounds
it could be considered only if broader simultaneous water quality problems justified a high-intensity treatment approach
potential_risks: high energy demand and membrane O&M are not well matched to the stated constraints
concentrate management creates a significant residuals burden
this option is unnecessarily complex for a single primary VOC contaminant case with otherwise typical groundwater quality
